text: Laparoscopic anterior resection for rectal neuroendocrine tumor.
This fifty six year old gentleman was diagnosed to have a rectal tumor on CT scan and colonoscopy.
It was found to be a tumor in the upper rectum.
The biopsy revealed grade one neuroendocrine tumor.
You can see the CT scan and the DOTA scan shows the high uptake in the rectal tumor with nodal metastasis in the mesorectum as shown here.
The patient is positioned for laparoscopic surgery. The surgeon begins dissection, mobilizing the redundant sigmoid colon from its attachment to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon exposes the left ureter, making it clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the upper rectal area and begins mobilizing it, likely using surgical instruments to separate the rectum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses an Ensil energy device to dissect tissue, focusing on precise separation and mobilization within the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the rectum and dissects the pre-sacral area, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue planes in this region.
The surgeon carefully preserves the pelvic nerves while continuing dissection on both the right and left sides of the pelvis, ensuring nerve integrity during the procedure.
Here the cameraman plays a very important role while assisting.
The surgeon carefully dissects and divides the lateral ligaments of the rectum, using precise movements to separate these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon utilizes monopolar diathermy, bipolar diathermy, and NCIL as energy sources to perform dissection in the pelvic region, with visible precision in separating tissue layers.
The surgeon completes dissection on both sides of the pre-sacral area using the NCIL device, with visible separation of tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon identifies small nodes within the mesorectum during the dissection process.
The surgeon has removed all nodes with the specimen, revealing a clearly dissected area as they proceed deeper into the pelvis in the pre-sacral region.
The surgeon focuses on the area of the pedicle, which is visible within the dissected pre-sacral region.
The surgeon carefully dissects the inferior mesenteric artery, using surgical instruments to separate it from surrounding tissues in the pre-sacral area.
The surgeon uses a hook and right angle instrument to dissect the area, then clips the artery with hemolog clips before dividing it.
The surgeon dissects the vein, clips it with hemolog clips, and divides it, following the previous dissection of the artery in the pelvic region.
The surgeon approaches the entry area of the resection, which appears nearly complete, with clear visualization of the dissected tissue planes in the pre-sacral area.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to carefully resect the area near the seminal vesicles, ensuring these structures are not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon uses endo staplers to transect the colon, followed by the use of a circular stapler to perform the anastomosis after the colon resection.
The resected specimen is displayed, showing the tissue that has been removed during the procedure.
Thank you.
